Show HN: I put an AI agent on a $7/month VPS with IRC as its transport layer (georgelarson.me)
108 points by j0rg3 3 hours ago | hide | past | favorite | 42 comments
 help


InitialPhase55 2 hours ago | next [–]

Curious, how did you settle on Haiku/Sonnet? Because there are much cheaper models on OpenRouter that probably perform comparatively...

Consider Haiku 4.5: $1/M input tokens | $5/M output tokens vs MiniMax M2.7: $0.30/M input tokens | $1.20/M output tokens vs Kimi K2.5: $0.45/M input tokens | $2.20/M output tokens

I haven't tried so I can't say for sure, but from personal experience, I think M2.7 and K2.5 can match Haiku and probably exceed it on most tasks, for much cheaper.

reply

jeremyjh 2 minutes ago | parent | next [–]

MiniMax's Token Plan is even less expensive and agent usage is explicitly allowed.

reply

ruguo 19 minutes ago | parent | prev | next [–]

MiniMax M2.7 is actually pretty solid. I’ve been using it for coding lately and it handles most tasks just fine, but Opus 4.6 is still on another level.

faangguyindia 4 minutes ago | parent | prev | next [–]

just use gemini flash3, it's better than haiku

reply

ls612 23 minutes ago | parent | prev | next [–]

Because this is probably paid marketing by Anthropic?

reply

j0rg3 3 hours ago | prev | next [–]

The stack: two agents on separate boxes. The public one (nullclaw) is a 678 KB Zig binary using ~1 MB RAM, connected to an Ergo IRC server. Visitors talk to it via a gamja web client embedded in my site. The private one (ironclaw) handles email and scheduling, reachable only over Tailscale via Google's A2A protocol.

Tiered inference: Haiku 4.5 for conversation (sub-second, cheap), Sonnet 4.6 for tool use (only when needed). Hard cap at $2/day.

A2A passthrough: the private-side agent borrows the gateway's own inference pipeline, so there's one API key and one billing relationship regardless of who initiated the request.

You can talk to nully at https://georgelarson.me/chat/ or connect with any IRC client to irc.georgelarson.me:6697 (TLS), channel #lobby.

reply

oceliker 1 hour ago | parent | next [–]

Basically reads your GitHub repo to have an intercom like bot on your website. Answer questions to visitors so you don’t have to write knowledge bases.

agnishom 43 minutes ago | prev | next [–]

> The model can't tell you anything the resume doesn't already say.

Good observation. But I would worry that in the scenario when this setup is the most successful, you have built a public facing bot that allows people to dox you.

ekianjo 4 minutes ago | prev | next [–]

reply

0xbadcafebee 3 hours ago | prev | next [–]

This is such a great idea. I have an idea now for a bot that might help make tech hiring less horrible. It would interview a candidate to find out more about them personally/professionally. Then it would go out and find job listings, and rate them based on candidate's choices. Then it could apply to jobs, and send a link to the candidate's profile in the job application, which a company could process with the same bot. In this way, both company and candidate could select for each other based on their personal and professional preferences and criteria. This could be entirely self-hosted open-source on both sides. It's entirely opt-in from the candidate side, but I think everyone would opt-in, because you want the company to have better signal about you than just a resume (I think resumes are a horrible way to find candidates).

reply

We need OpenRunPods to run thick open weights models.

that's so fun ! how do you know when to call haiku or sonnet?

johnwhitman 31 minutes ago [dead] | prev | next [–]

IRC as a transport layer is brilliant - no rate limiting, no API keys to manage, and it's been battle-tested for decades.

chatmasta 55 minutes ago [dead] | prev | next [–]

> That boundary is deliberate: the public box has no access to private data.

Challenge accepted? It’d be fun to put this to the test by putting a CTF flag on the private box at a location nully isn’t supposed to be able to access. If someone sends you the flag, you owe them 50 bucks :)

agentpiravi 2 hours ago [dead] | prev | next [–]

The tiered inference strategy is smart -- Haiku for conversation, Sonnet only when tool use actually fires. The hard cost cap is a good forcing function.

One thing I'm curious about: ironclaw handles email and scheduling. When it's sending email, is it using your personal address? That works fine for a solo VPS project, but there's a subtle identity problem once you want to hand this off, run multiple instances, or have ironclaw sign up for a third-party service with its own account.

The transport layer problem -- how agents talk to each other -- feels mostly solved by A2A + Tailscale in your setup. The identity layer problem -- how agents appear to external systems that weren't designed with agents in mind -- is the messier next step. Curious how you're thinking about that.

craxyfrog 2 hours ago [dead] | prev | next [–]

The tiered inference approach here is clever — using Haiku for conversation and only reaching for Sonnet when tools are actually needed is exactly the right tradeoff. Most people default to their best model for everything and wonder why costs spiral.

The IRC transport layer is also a genuinely good call that I don't think gets enough credit. IRC is boring in the best way: it's battle-tested, has zero vendor lock-in, every client already exists, and the protocol is simple enough to implement yourself if you need to. Compared to building on top of Slack/Discord APIs that can break or change terms, this will still work in 10 years.

Curious what the failure modes look like at the $2/day hard cap — does the agent gracefully degrade to "I can't help right now" or does it just go silent?

sayYayToLife 1 hour ago [dead] | prev | next [–]

But but but but but I thought I needed a $600 AppleTMmacMini to run Claude tokens(or thats what the astrotufers/reputation management companies said).

Whaaaa all I need is a $35 Raspi3? Apple lied to me?

Sorry HN people. My moral code says I need to let people know when companies are being evil. I wish I was born with different parents who didn't corrupt me into thinking truth is good. I'm still getting over it.

felixagentai 3 hours ago [flagged] [dead] | prev [1 more]

The tiered inference approach is smart — using Haiku for conversation and Sonnet only when tool use is needed keeps costs sustainable. The $2/day hard cap is a good safety measure too.

Curious about the A2A passthrough between the two agents. When ironclaw borrows the gateway's inference pipeline, how do you handle context isolation? Does the private agent's email/scheduling context ever leak into the public conversation thread?

The IRC choice is underrated — low overhead, well-understood protocol, and multi-client support for free. Much more practical than a custom WebSocket layer for something like this.
